**Sources:** 

1. 4. [https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/a-generative-ai-reset-rewiring-to-turn-potential-into-value-in-2024](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/a-generative-ai-reset-rewiring-to-turn-potential-into-value-in-2024)

Now reading all one by one 

### **Article 1** 

**Moving past gen AI’s honeymoon phase: Seven hard truths for CIOs to get from pilot to scale**

**The value gen AI could generate is transformational. But capturing the full extent of that value will come only when companies harness gen AI at scale.**

[https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/moving-past-gen-ais-honeymoon-phase-seven-hard-truths-for-cios-to-get-from-pilot-to-scale](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/moving-past-gen-ais-honeymoon-phase-seven-hard-truths-for-cios-to-get-from-pilot-to-scale)  
**Scaling Gen AI for the Shaper approach** 

* Building Gen AI pilots is now easy, but turning them into at-scale capabilities is the challenge  
* Needed → Creating Gen AI capability into business value   
* At present only 15% of orgs say that Gen AI capability is making meaningful impact to their bottomline   
* **Step 1:** Target those use cases that have **High** **business impact** and **High technical feasibility** 

**![][image1]**

* **Business Impact \-**   
  * Value creation can be quantified   
    * Strategic alignment \- There is alignment with business strategy   
    * Ease of adoption \- end users are enthusiastic about adopting the solution   
    * Business readiness \- business can take up these initiatives   
  * **Technical Feasibility**   
    * Data readiness \- data is available, and doesn’t need to be created or synthesised, there is clarity on how to handle sensitive data  
    * Solution readiness  
    * Ability to scale \- as the users and cloud consumption increases, will the business model remain viable  
    * Reusability \- can the solution be repurposed   
* **Step 2: Orchestration \- can the interactions and integrations for each use case be orchestrated cohesively** 

**![][image2]**

* **Things to orchestration:**  
  * each use case needs to access multiple models, databases, prompt libraries and applications  
  * **sources \-   applications and databases** on cloud, on-prem, with a vendor   
  * Protocols like RBAC, policies  
  * Additional components  
* **Effective orchestration \-** is embedding the organization’s domain and workflow expertise into the management of the step-by-step flow and sequencing of the model, data, and system interactions of an application running on a cloud foundation.  
* **core component of an effective orchestration engine \-**  
  * **An API gateway \-** which authenticates users, ensures compliance, logs request-and-response pairs and routes requests to the best models, including those offered by third parties  
    * Enables cost tracking   
    * Provides usage monitoring for risk and compliance teams   
    * Gateway helps teams to scale while allowing teams to operate independently   
  * **Effective end-to-end automation \- everything from**  
    * Data wrangling (cleaning and integration), data pipeline construction, model monitoring and risk review  
  * **A modern MLOps platform \-**  to manage the automated flow, ensure efficient use of cloud resources, accelerate TTM  
  * **Observability tools \-** Gen AI tools can produce inconsistent results, so the orchestration capability cannot be running in the background unchecked, so it is important to do develop **hyperattentive observing and triaging capabilities** \- **Observability tools** monitor the gen AI application’s interactions with users in real time, tracking metrics such as response time, accuracy, and user satisfaction scores. If an application begins to generate inaccurate or inappropriate responses, the tool alerts the development team to investigate and make any necessary adjustments to the model parameters, prompt templates, or orchestration flow.  
* **Step 3: Cost management \- Gen AI data usage and model interactions costs can spiral out of control \- need to understand what drives costs**   
  * **Change management costs \-  is the biggest cost \-**  
    * **Traditional digital solution change management costs** used to be $1 for development to $1 for change management   
    * **Gen AI change management costs** are $1 for development to $3 for change management   
    *  **Change actions include** \-   
      * training people to understand potential value and risk of genAI,   
      * role modeling,   
      * active performance tracking  
      * strong performance management infrastructure   
      * Involving end users in solution development   
      * Involving domain experts to ensure models and applications understand company’s context and internal data   
  * **Run costs \-**   
    * Are bigger than build costs for genAI   
    * It is much more expensive to run GenAI applications than to build them   
    * Foundation model usage and labor costs → run costs  
    * Model and data pipeline maintenance ( labour costs)  
    * Risk and compliance management also contributes to the run costs   
  * **Drive down model costs** \- by looking at Ai architecture and using an array of cost reduction tools and capabilities   
  * **Looking at ROI \-** develop a modeling discipline that instills an ROI focus on every gen AI use case without getting lost in endless rounds of analysis.  
* **Step 4: Tame the proliferation of tools and tech**   
  * Teams will push their own usecase, their own environment, requiring orgs to support multiple infrastructures, LLMs, tools and approaches   
  * **“too many platforms”** is the top technology **obstacle** to implementing gen AI at scale  
  * more infrastructures and tools, the higher the complexity and cost of operations, which in turn makes scaled rollouts unfeasible.  
  * To get to scale, companies **need a manageable set of tools and infrastructures**.  
  * It is better to avoid over analysis as the LLMs continue to become a commodity  
* **Step 5 \- build teams that can take it beyond the IT function and embed it into the business**  
  * Build a **Centre of Excellence t**o prioritize use cases, allocate resources and monitor performance   
  * centralized function **enables close collaboration** between technology, business, and risk leads  
  * is disciplined in followin**g proven protocols for driving successful programs** \- like QBRs and interventions to resolve issues, reallocate resources, or shut down poor-performing initiatives.  
  * The central governing structure should ensure that **effective risk protocols** are implemented and followed  
  * E.g. e**nsuring that Build teams map the potential risks associated with each use case**; and implement technical and “human-in-the-loop” protocols throughout the use-case life cycle.  
  * This oversight body also needs to **manage gen AI risk by assessing exposures and implementing mitigating strategies.**  
  * to cluster related use cases to ensure large-scale impact and drive large ideas  
  * to act as the guardians for value, not just managers of work.  
  * put in place clearly defined governance protocols for senior management.

**Example of a CoE** : One financial services company put in place clearly defined governance protocols for senior management. 

* **A steering group**, sponsored by the CIO and chief strategy officer, focused on enterprise governance, strategy, and communication, driving use-case identification and approvals.   
* **An enablement group,** sponsored by the CTO, focused on decisions around data architecture, data science, data engineering, and building core enabling capabilities.   
* The CTO also mandated that **at least one experienced architect join a use-case team early** in their process to ensure the team used the established standards and tool sets. This **oversight and governance clarity was crucial** in helping the business go from managing just five to more than 50 use cases in its pipeline.

* **Step 6: Data accuracy \- do NOT assume that GenAI can sweep the right data and also make sense of it \-**   
  * High performing GenAI solutions need clean and accurate data   
  * Investing in data foundations to generate good data is important   
  * investing in targeted labeling—particularly for the data used for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)—can have a significant impact on the quality of answers to gen AI queries.  
  * it’s critical to invest the time to grade the importance of content sources (“authority weighting”), which helps the model understand the relative value of different sources \- this requires significant human oversight from people with relevant expertise.  
  * **Example** \- At a materials science product company, for example, various teams accessed product information, but each one had a different version. R\&D had materials safety sheets, application engineering teams (tech sales/support teams) developed their own version to find solutions for unique client calls, commercialization teams had product descriptions, and customer support teams had a set of specific product details to answer queries. As each team updated its version of the product information, conflicts emerged, making it difficult for gen AI models to use the data. To address this issue, the company is putting all relevant product information in one place.

* **Step 7 \- Reusable code**   
  * Reusable code can increase the development speed of generative AI use cases by 30 to 50 percent.  
  * But in their haste to make meaningful breakthroughs, teams often focus on individual use cases, which sinks any hope for scale. CIOs need to shift the business’s energies to building solutions that can serve many use cases.  
  * In committing to reusability, **however**, it is **easy to get caught in building abstract gen AI capabilities** that don’t get used, even though, technically, it would be easy to do so.  
  * **A more effective way to build up reusable assets is to do a disciplined review of a set of use cases, typically three to five**, to ascertain their common needs or functions. Teams can then build these common elements as assets or modules that can be easily reused or strung together to create a new capability.  
  * **Data preprocessing and ingestion, f**or example, could include a data-chunking mechanism, a structured data-and-metadata loader, and a data transformer as distinct modules.

### Article 2 

**Technology’s generational moment with generative AI: A CIO and CTO guide**

**CIOs and CTOs can take nine actions to reimagine business and technology with generative AI.**

[https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/technologys-generational-moment-with-generative-ai-a-cio-and-cto-guide](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/technologys-generational-moment-with-generative-ai-a-cio-and-cto-guide)

McKinsey have identified nine actions all technology leaders can take to create value, orchestrate technology and data, scale solutions, and manage risk for generative AI

**Action 1 \- Determine the company’s posture for adoption of Gen AI**

* Build consensus on the level of risk the org is comfortable with   
* Identify how GenAI fits into the org’s overall business strategy   
* Determine company wide policies and guidelines   
* Access to user-friendly guides to those policies and guidelines and comms about GenAI

**Action 2 \- Identify use cases that build value through improved productivity, growth and new business models** 

* Identify the most valuable opportunities and issues across the org that can benefit from GenAI  
* Discuss how to cluster use cases either by domain or use case type to get most value   
* Take into account the limitations of talent and capabilities to identify feasibility and resource needs  
* Set up a FInAI capability to estimate true costs and ROI in GenAI use cases   
* Take into account various aspects of GenAI costs \- model and vendor costs, model interactions, ongoing usage fee, human oversight costs 

**Action 3 \- Reimagine the Technology function** 

* **Software development and testing can be expedited with the help of GenAI** \- which means this should be leveraged \- which means developers need to be trained to leverage these benefits, integrations and deployment pipelines will need to be automated with DevSecOps practices   
* **Technical Debt \-**  normally accounts for 20% to 40% of technology budgets and significantly slow the pace of development \- Gen Ai can be used to address tech debt by using code refactoring and automated test case generation capabilities of GenAI  
* **IT Operations (ITOps) \-** GenAI can improve productivity by automating tasks and processes in the ITOps space \- e.g., handling requests like password resets, status requests, triage and resolution processes, improving observability, developing documentation etc. 

**Action 4 \- Take advantage of existing services or adapt open-source generative AI models** 

* **Similar to rent, buy or build decisions** when it comes to strategies for developing genAI capabilities. **There are 3 options be a Taker, Shaper or Maker**  
*  **Taker** **organisations**  
  * uses publicly available models through a chat interface or API with minimal customisation, e.g. Github Copilot to generate code  
  * Simplest in terms of engineering and infra needs   
  * Fastest to get up and running   
* **Shaper organisations**  
  * Integrates models with internal data and systems to generate more customized results   
  * E.g., model that connects GenAI tools to CRM system   
  * E.g. fine tuning a model with internal company documents to act as a chatbot   
  * Companies that need to scale GenAI capabilities, or develop more proprietary capabilities etc need to be Shapers \- there are 2 ways to be shapers \-   
  * 1\. Bring the model to the data \- the model is hosted on the organization’s infrastructure, either on-premises or in the cloud environment.  
  * 2\. bring data to the model,” where an organization can aggregate its data and deploy a copy of the large model on cloud infrastructure.  
* **Maker** **organisations**  
  * Builds foundation models to address discrete business case  
  * Requires huge volumes of data, deep expertise, massive compute power  
  * Requires substantial one-off investment

**Action 5: Upgrade your enterprise technology architecture** 

* Orgs use many genAI models of varying size, complexity and capability   
* To generate value, these models need to work together with existing systems and applications   
* For the Taker archetype, this level of coordination isn’t necessary. But for companies looking to scale the advantages of generative AI as Shapers or Makers, CIOs and CTOs need to upgrade their technology architecture.  
* The prime goal is to integrate generative AI models into internal systems and enterprise applications and to build pipelines to various data sources.  
* Recent advances in integration and orchestration frameworks, such as LangChain and LlamaIndex, have significantly reduced the effort required to connect different generative AI models with other applications and data sources.  
* Tech leaders will need to define reference architectures and standard integration patterns for their organization (such as standard API formats and parameters that identify the user and the model invoking the API).  
* 5 key elements of architecture \- Context management and caching, Policy Management, Model hub, Prompt library, MLOps platform

![][image3]

![][image4]

**Action 6 \- Develop a data architecture to enable access to quality data**

* To generate and scale value, To bring cost reductions and improved data and knowledge protection \- an org needs to know how to take value from its own data   
* Means it needs **data architecture that connects GenAI models to internal data sources** which help provide context or help fine-tune the models to create more relevant outputs   
* **Create comprehensive data architecture** for both structured and unstructured data sources   
* **Optimise data for GenAI use \-** adding metadata to improve traceability, augment training data with synthetic data to improve diversity, improving data quality   
* **Ensure existing Infra and cloud services** can support data volumes and  storage needs  
* **Prioritise development of data pipelines \-** to connect GenAi models to relevant data sources 

**Action 7 \- Create a Centralized cross-platform GenAI platform team** 

* first step is setting up a generative AI platform team whose core focus is developing and maintaining a platform service where approved generative AI models can be provisioned on demand for use by product and application teams.  
* platform team also defines protocols for how generative AI models integrate with internal systems, enterprise applications, and tools  
* The platform team also develops and implements standardized approaches to manage risk, such as responsible AI frameworks  
* Ensure platform team is staffed with people who have the right skills

**Action 8: Tailor upskilling programs by roles and proficiency levels** 

* Now GenAI has the potential to massively uplift employee productivity and capability, but the benefits are not evenly visible due to different proficiency levels of the people using those GenAI capabilities   
* **E.g.** while using GenAI Highly skilled developers saw gains of up to 50 to 80 percent, while junior developers experienced a 7 to 10 percent decline in speed.  
* These disparities underscore the need for technology leaders, working with the chief human resources officer (CHRO), to rethink their talent management strategy to build the workforce of the future.


**Action 9:  Evaluate the new risk landscape and establish ongoing mitigation practices**

* Generative AI presents a fresh set of ethical questions and risks,  
  *  including “hallucinations,” whereby the generative AI model presents an incorrect response based on the highest-probability response;   
  * the accidental release of confidential personally identifiable information;   
  * inherent bias in the large data sets the models use;   
  * and high degrees of uncertainty related to intellectual property (IP).  
* Addressing this new landscape requires a significant review of cyber practices   
* and updating the software development process to evaluate risk and identify mitigation actions **before** model development begins  
* Early generative AI use cases should focus on areas where the cost of error is low, to allow the organization to work through inevitable setbacks and incorporate learnings.  
* **To protect data privacy,**   
  * establish and enforce sensitive data tagging protocols,   
  * set up data access controls in different domains  
  * add extra protection when data is used externally,   
  * and include privacy safeguards.

**Extra \- Risk mitigation actions for Handling hallucinations:** 

* adjusting the level of creativity (known as the “temperature”) of a model when it generates responses;   
* augmenting the model with relevant internal data to provide more context;   
* using libraries that impose guardrails on what can be generated;   
* using “moderation” models to check outputs;   
* and adding clear disclaimers.

### **Article 3**

**Agentic AI: Moving beyond pilots to enterprise impact** 

[https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-live/webinars/agentic-ai-moving-beyond-pilots-to-enterprise-impact](https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-live/webinars/agentic-ai-moving-beyond-pilots-to-enterprise-impact)

Capturing the value of agentic AI requires wielding the power of an agentic AI mesh—a composable, distributed, and governed architecture that enables multiple agents to reason, collaborate, and act autonomously across systems in a secure and scalable way. The agentic AI mesh becomes the connective tissue of the enterprise; it coordinates the work of multiple agents, integrates with core systems, and evolves along with the technology itself.

**Resetting a company’s approach to AI transformation is no simple task. Among the requirements:**

* **An enterprise-wide AI/agentic strategy**. Senior leaders are aligned with the strategy, and enterprise-level programs tie directly to the company’s biggest priorities. Agents are used to reimagine business models and create new sources of value as well as propel efficiency.  
* These critical capabilities:  
  * **People**. Employees are equipped for a world of human–agent collaboration. Some companies have introduced new roles such as agent orchestrator and human-in-the-loop designer.  
  * **Governance**. A strong governance framework defines agents’ autonomy levels, decision boundaries, and overnight mechanisms.  
  * **Tech architecture**. Over time, enterprise architecture will be reshaped by agents as they become the connective tissue of day-to-day operations.  
  * **Data**. Agents can only be as effective as the data they rely on.  
* **Adoption, scaling, and implementation.** To attain industrialized, scalable delivery, systems for integration and monitoring are thoroughly thought through in advance and designed for efficiency from day one. Systems are technically and economically sustainable and allow the company to react to changes with agility.

**![][image5]**

### Article 4 

**LLM to ROI: How to scale gen AI in retail \- August 5, 2024 | Article**  
[**https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/llm-to-roi-how-to-scale-gen-ai-in-retail**](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/retail/our-insights/llm-to-roi-how-to-scale-gen-ai-in-retail)

* gen AI is poised to unlock between $240 billion to $390 billion in economic value for retailers  
* **Retailers faced the following challenges to implementing AI widely \-**   
  * Insufficencient tech capabilities and talent  
  * Data quality   
  * privacy concerns  
  * Implementation expenses  
* A small number of retailers did **succeed in harnessing genAI’s power**. They did 2 things:   
  * **Focus on key domains:** They **considered how gen AI use cases can help transform specific domains** rather than spreading their resources too thin across a range of scenarios.  
  * **Org wide changes:** they effectively transitioned from pilot and proof-of-concept to deployment at scale. This requires not just data prioritization and technological integration but also significant organizational changes to support widespread AI adoption.  
* **Gen AI advantages to retailers:**  
  * Streamline operations  
  * Faster and better decisions across internal value chains  
  * Efficiency gains  
  * Redefine decision making  
  * Reinvent the customer experience   
  * Deepen relationship with customers  
  * Improved customer experience   
* 2 ways GenAI can transform retail   
  * Augmenting retail’s internal value chain  
  * Reinventing the customer experience   
    

**1 Augmenting Retail’s Internal Value chain** 

* **End to end Retail value chain includes** \- Procurement, Distribution, In-store operations, E-Commerce, Marketing, Back office, Commercial  
* **Identify quick win use cases that strengthen your core value proposition for the market you are serving**   
* gen AI can also help retailers accelerate their decision making by **automatically generating insights, root causes, and domain-level and company-wide responses**  
* **Gen AI and advanced analytics to create Personalised reports to help analyse**\- competitors’ tactics, changes in pricing and promotion, supply chain issues, and unexpected disruptions, analysis for sales declines and help to devise strategies to avoid future sales drops  
* Examples \-   
  * **Marketing**. Amazon launched an AI-powered image generation tool in late 2023 to help advertisers deliver a better ad experience. The tool uses gen AI text prompts to transform basic product photos into more realistic lifestyle images.  
  * **In-store operations**. In June 2023, Lindex, a Swedish retailer, announced the release of the “Lindex Copilot” to support its store employees. The tool, which is trained on the company’s sales and store data, provides employees with personalized advice and guidance about store operations and information about daily tasks.

 ![][image6]

**2 Reinventing Customer experience** 

* Generative AI can make the customer journey more efficient, for both retailer and shopper  
* **Gen-AI-powered chatbot assistants \-**  
  * to better engage with customers.   
  * Customers can use chatbots to receive product recommendations,   
  * learn more about a product or retailer,   
  * or add or remove items from their virtual shopping carts.  
  * Importantly, since many consumers will use these chatbots before deciding to purchase a product rather than after, using chatbots allows retailers to engage with customers earlier in their shopping journey, which can help increase customers’ overall satisfaction.  
* **How chatbots work**   
  * Gen AI chatbot’s LLM system recognises the intent of customer’s message   
  * The LLM system then connects to the various product and other datasets   
  * LLM system also connects to other models like personalisation engine to recommend  
  * **Frequent user testing is needed to calibrate the response**  
  * **Dedicated resources need to be allocated toward product design**  
  * Chatbots thus helps to reduce customer’s mental load and shopping time  
  * **Chatbots can be further deeply personalised** by being able to remember customer order history, product preferences and shopping habits

**Retailer org and the Maker Shaper Taker archetypes** 

* **companies will need to decide** if they are   
  * a **“taker” (a user of preexisting tools),**   
  * a “**shaper”** (an integrator of available models with proprietary data for more customized results),   
  * or a “**maker**” (a builder of foundation models).  
* **Across the internal value chain** \- most retailers will likely adopt the **taker** archetype, using publicly available interfaces or APIs with little to no customization to meet their needs \- **but these solutions do not provide a way for retailers to fully realise the technology value**   
* At the same time, **most retailers won’t be able to adopt the maker archetype**, given that the costs associated with building foundation models are outside the typical retailer’s budget.\\

**Challenges faced by retailers in GenAI implementations (specifically chatbots)**

* How much it (chatbot) will **cost**   
* How much it will **cost to enhance / shape** it   
* **Length of conversation** is **inversely** proportional to **degree of chatbot personalisation**  
* **Better chatbot \== better purchase conversion**   
* **Cost of LLM APIs** \- btw AI experts believe that the price of LLM APIs will continue to drop substantially, with some estimates showing a drop of as much as 80 percent within the next two to three years.  
* Business case based on McKinsey cases studies \- a 2 to 4 percent basket uplift can justify LLM cost, but sometimes other factors can be added to justify the business case   
* Factor in the **alternate cost of attracting new customers**   
* Factor in the **benefit of higher purchase frequency with existing customers** 

**Measuring GenAI (chatBot) impact**

* When comparing a traditional retailer app with the minimum viable product of a gen-AI-enabled chatbot, the chatbot reduced the time spent to complete an order by 50 to 70 percent.

**Chatbot vs Smart search** \- 

* smart-search tools powered by gen AI can better understand the context and intent of a search term, even if it veers away from keyword use.   
* Although these smart-search tools may be limited in functionality vs chatbots  
* Smart search tools are limited in impact   
* they are easier and less expensive to develop.   
* carry fewer risks compared with using a chatbot;   
* their outputs are generally limited to a list of products rather than longer text that a chatbot would give,   
* which means the responses are less likely to be harmful, offensive, or inaccurate.

**How to scale the use of gen AI in retail \- 5 imperatives to outcompete**

* Identify domain-level transformation candidates   
* Upskill talent to develop gen AI skills  
* Form a centralized, cross-functional team to enable scaling  
* Set up technology architecture to scale  
* Ensure data quality to fuel models

Article 5   
**A data leader’s technical guide to scaling gen AI**  
[https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/a-data-leaders-technical-guide-to-scaling-gen-ai](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/a-data-leaders-technical-guide-to-scaling-gen-ai)

**three actions for data and AI leaders to move from gen AI pilots to scaling data solutions.** 

1. **strengthen the quality and readiness of their data for gen AI use cases.**   
2. **use gen AI to build better data products with modernized data platforms.**   
3. **key data-management considerations to reuse and accelerate the development of data solutions.**

* **Managing data remains one of the main barriers** to value creation from gen AI.   
* 70 percent of top performers in a recent McKinsey survey said they have experienced **difficulties integrating data into AI models**,   
* ranging from issues with **data quality**, defining **processes for data governance**, and **having sufficient training data**.

**Improve data quality** 

* There is high complexity in handling unstructured data sets  
* Processing both structured and unstructured data needs that SME knowledge to be codified into the system   
* Knowledge graphs help to map data points from unstructured data sets to structured data sets   
* Different methods are needed to understand different data types (e.g. word, pdf, powerpoint)  
* Multimodal models help to parse a variety of data types   
* Accuracy issues require constant review   
* **Data leaders need to focus resources on implementing automated evaluation methods, mechanisms to manage versioning, and data-relevancy scoring to enhance multimodal model output accuracy and consistency.**  
* **Example \-**  In order to use product information from structured and unstructured data sources, it had to build data pipelines for parsing and processing unstructured data, identify which version of each document was most recent, and adapt the length of articles for mobile users. The firm’s data engineers used multimodal model capabilities to parse tabular data from documents into structured data and build a medallion architecture (a popular design pattern for organizing data that supports modular pipeline development). Additionally, they introduced versioning and relevancy scores to improve output accuracy. As a result, the company was able to quickly start work on use cases, such as due-diligence activities, with a production-grade gen AI environment within two weeks.

**Use gen AI to build better data products with modernized data platforms**

* At times GenAI use cases face data challenges \- because the required data are difficult to obtain and process  
* data and AI leaders are investing in GenAI tools to generate synthetic data as test data or to produce new values based completely  
* Use gen AI to accelerate the building of reusable data products and   
* automatically generating both end-to-end data transformation pipelines   
* Shift to end-to-end creation of data products   
* building data pipelines by automating all the steps,   
* time savings of 80 to 90 percent and enhanced scalability for specific use cases.

**Enhance consistency with better orchestration and data management**

* Before migrating data products and their underlying pipelines from one platform to another, companies need to first determine the right LLM for the job.  
* By understanding which LLMs to use for given coding languages, and how to automate code translation across languages, companies can better migrate pipelines from mainframes and legacy-managed services already in the cloud to more-modern cloud resources.

**Scale gen AI with security and coding standards \-** check proliferation of tools, ensure alignment with enterprise architecture, automating the integration of coding best practices

**Protect data at each step \-** 

* AI leaders are designing modularized pipelines capable of automatically securing data  
* build consistent, secure access controls and guardrails at each checkpoint in the data pipeline,  
* from ingestion to vectorization to retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to consumption by gen AI models.

### **Article 6**

**A generative AI reset: Rewiring to turn potential into value in 2024**  
[https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jj6e0i3G9Hmu73advXo3bxeBLcUfrl2WFKkpYBzjwCM/edit?tab=t.0\#heading=h.y9h44ct9z773](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jj6e0i3G9Hmu73advXo3bxeBLcUfrl2WFKkpYBzjwCM/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.y9h44ct9z773) 

**Mckinsey book: Rewired:** provides a detailed manual on the six capabilities needed to deliver the kind of broad change that harnesses digital and AI technology. In this article, we will explore how to extend each of those capabilities to implement a successful gen AI program at **scale.**[https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-on-books/rewired-first-edition](https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-on-books/rewired-first-edition) 

**Next: How to evaluate and justify business cases for AI implementations**  
[https://docs.google.com/document/d/1W5CIqDT2xyFfz5BpfllAyC8hSjeG3uHjzAvppPXibBA/edit?tab=t.0](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1W5CIqDT2xyFfz5BpfllAyC8hSjeG3uHjzAvppPXibBA/edit?tab=t.0)

* Only at-scale AI solutions can create competitive advantage for organisations   
* This requires making changes to the way things actually get done the   
* Including but not limited to   
  * Working with the business to develop the strategic vision  
  * Scanning domains opportunities and use case across the org that would give strategic advantage   
  * implementing the road map for the use cases  
  * Placing cross-functional product teams with shared objectives and incentives to build AI tools   
  * Set up data and AI training for the enterprise to work better with data and AI tools   
  * Selecting LLM and cloud providers that could meet the needs of the enterprise  
* **Additionally orgs need to figure out where GenAI can give real competitive advantage**   
  * This needs the leaders to focus on the core business and not the aspects of the company that are a commodity capability   
  * Understand the Taker, Shaper and Maker approaches to AI  
  * Understanding that the efficiencies in commodity capabilities can be achieved by being a Taker of GenAI tech and tools already out there  \- i.e., a user of available tools, often via APIs and subscription services  
  * Understand that efficiencies in strategic capabilities needs a Shaper approach \- i.e., an integrator of available models with proprietary data  
  * So the sweet spot for businesses is implementing a taker model for productivity improvements while building shaper applications for competitive advantage.  
  * Much of gen AI’s near-term value is closely tied to its ability to help people do their current jobs better → so productivity gains   
* **Another things orgs need to do is** figuring out how to generate revenue from increased productivity from GenAI \- Defining the plans for how to generate revenue from the increased productivity up front, therefore, is crucial to capturing the value.  
* **Another things to do \-** **Upskill the talent you have** but be clear about the gen-AI-specific skills you need  
  * Building up skills orgs can set up apprenticeship, training people to then train others, and building communities of practitioners  
* **Another things orgs need to do \- Form a centralized team to establish standards that enable responsible scaling**  
  * to develop and put in place protocols and standards to support scale,   
  * ensuring that teams can access models while also   
  * minimizing risk and containing costs.   
  * procuring models and prescribing ways to access them,   
  * developing standards for data readiness,   
  * setting up approved prompt libraries,   
  * and allocating resources.  
* **Another thing orgs need to do: Set up the technology architecture to scale**  
  * Building for scale doesn’t mean building a new technology architecture.  
  * But it does mean focusing on a few core decisions that simplify and speed up processes  
  * **Focus on reusing your technology. Reusing code** can increase the development speed of gen AI use cases by 30 to 50 percent.  
  * **Focus the architecture on enabling efficient connections between gen AI models and internal systems**  
    * enable access to a business’s data and applications  
    * Enable advances in integration and orchestration frameworks   
    * laying out what those integrations are and how to enable them is critical to ensure these models work efficiently and to avoid the complexity that creates technical debt  
    * Build up your testing and quality assurance capabilities.  
* **Another thing orgs need to do \- Ensure data quality and focus on unstructured data to fuel your models that use large scale and scope of data**   
  * from **clarifying decision rights** to   
  * **defining clear data processes** to   
  * **establishing taxonomies so models can access the data they need**.  
  * **Understand what value is locked into your unstructured data** \- the real value from LLMs comes from their ability to work with unstructured data (for example, PowerPoint slides, videos, and text) \-   
  * **map out which unstructured data sources are most valuable and establish metadata tagging standards** so models can process the data and teams can find what they need (tagging is particularly important to help companies remove data from models as well, if necessary).  
  * **Optimize to lower costs at scale \- companies scaling their proofs of concept without optimizing their data approach can be paying 10times more**   
    * **Cost 1 \- storage costs** arising from companies uploading terabytes of data into the cloud and wanting that data available 24/7 \- you done time need all data to be available all the time \- identify how much is critical data that needs to be highly available, and reduce the latency of other data to 24 to 48 hour period   
    * **Cost 2** \- **Computation costs on cloud platforms-** storage costs arising from companies uploading terabytes of data into the cloud and wanting that data available 24/7 \- when companies are building their own models (the maker archetype) but also when they are using pretrained models and running them with their own data and use cases (the shaper archetype)  
* **Another thing orgs need to do \- Build trust and reusability to drive adoption and scale**  
  * **invest extra time and money to build trust by ensuring model accuracy and making it easy to check answers**  
  * identify all the **guardrails** to put in place  
  * **help teams to understand the limitations of model**s   
  * **Train teams on how best to get the right answers \-** by providing context, by more specific prompting  
  * **stop building one-off solutions** that are hard to use for other similar use cases.

**Extra: What do orgs with great Gen AI technical talent have:** 

* best gen AI technical talent has **design skills to uncover where to focus solutions,**   
* **contextual understanding** to ensure the most relevant and high-quality answers are generated,   
* **collaboration skills to work well with knowledge experts (**to test and validate answers and develop an appropriate curation approach),   
* **strong forensic skills to figure out causes of breakdowns** (is the issue the data, the interpretation of the user’s intent, the quality of metadata on embeddings, or something else?),   
* **and anticipation skills to conceive of and plan for possible outcomes** and to put the right kind of tracking into their code.

**Extra: Copilot examples across three generative AI archetypes**

* “Taker” copilots help real estate customers sift through property options and find the most promising one, write code for a developer, and summarize investor transcripts.  
* “Shaper” copilots provide recommendations to sales reps for upselling customers by connecting generative AI tools to customer relationship management systems, financial systems, and customer behavior histories; create virtual assistants to personalize treatments for patients; and recommend solutions for maintenance workers based on historical data.  
* “Maker” copilots are foundation models that lab scientists at pharmaceutical companies can use to find and test new and better drugs more quickly.

**Extra: Skill composition of squads developing genAI solutions**  
For teams developing gen AI solutions, squad composition will be similar to AI teams but with

* data engineers and data scientists with gen AI experience   
* more contributors from risk management, compliance, and legal functions.
